From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Central Semitic language

This article is about the general language (macrolanguage). For specific varieties of Arabic and other uses, see [Arab (disambiguation)](/wiki/Arab_(disambiguation) "Arab (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic") or [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic").

**Arabic**[[c]](#cite_note-14) is a [Central Semitic language](/wiki/Central_Semitic_languages "Central Semitic languages") of the [Afroasiatic language family](/wiki/Afroasiatic_languages "Afroasiatic languages") spoken primarily in the [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world").[[12]](#cite_note-Al-Jallad-15) The [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") (ISO) assigns language codes to 32 [varieties of Arabic](/wiki/Varieties_of_Arabic "Varieties of Arabic"), including its standard form of Literary Arabic, known as [Modern Standard Arabic](/wiki/Modern_Standard_Arabic "Modern Standard Arabic"),[[13]](#cite_note-www-01.sil.org-16) which is derived from [Classical Arabic](/wiki/Classical_Arabic "Classical Arabic"). This distinction exists primarily among Western linguists; Arabic speakers themselves generally do not distinguish between Modern Standard Arabic and Classical Arabic, but rather refer to both as *al-ʿarabiyyatu l-fuṣḥā* (اَلعَرَبِيَّةُ ٱلْفُصْحَىٰ[[14]](#cite_note-17) "the eloquent Arabic") or simply **al-fuṣḥā** (اَلْفُصْحَىٰ).

Arabic is the [third–most widespread official language](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_the_number_of_countries_in_which_they_are_recognized_as_an_official_language "List of languages by the number of countries in which they are recognized as an official language") after English and French,[[15]](#cite_note-18) one of six [official languages of the United Nations](/wiki/Official_languages_of_the_United_Nations "Official languages of the United Nations"),[[16]](#cite_note-auto1-19) and the [liturgical language](/wiki/Sacred_language "Sacred language") of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam").[[17]](#cite_note-auto2-20) Arabic is widely taught in schools and universities around the world and is used to varying degrees in workplaces, governments and the media.[[17]](#cite_note-auto2-20) Arabic is spoken by as many as 380 million speakers, both native and non-native, in the Arab world,[[1]](#cite_note-e28-1) making it the [fifth–most spoken language](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_total_number_of_speakers "List of languages by total number of speakers") in the world[[18]](#cite_note-21) and the fourth–most used language on the Internet in terms of users.[[19]](#cite_note-22)[[20]](#cite_note-23) It also serves as the liturgical language of more than two billion [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims").[[16]](#cite_note-auto1-19) In 2011, *[Bloomberg Businessweek](/wiki/Bloomberg_Businessweek "Bloomberg Businessweek")* ranked Arabic the fourth–most useful language for business, after English, [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Mandarin_Chinese "Standard Mandarin Chinese"), and French.[[21]](#cite_note-24) Arabic is written with the [Arabic alphabet](/wiki/Arabic_alphabet "Arabic alphabet"), an [abjad](/wiki/Abjad "Abjad") script that is written from [right to left](/wiki/Right-to-left "Right-to-left").

During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), Arabic was a major vehicle of culture and learning, especially in science, mathematics and philosophy. As a result, many European languages have [borrowed](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") words from it. Arabic influence, mainly in vocabulary, is seen in [European languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Europe "Languages of Europe") (mainly [Spanish](/wiki/Arabic_language_influence_on_the_Spanish_language "Arabic language influence on the Spanish language") and to a lesser extent [Portuguese](/wiki/Influence_of_Arabic_on_other_languages#Portuguese "Influence of Arabic on other languages"), [Catalan](/wiki/Influence_of_Arabic_on_other_languages#Catalan "Influence of Arabic on other languages"), and [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language")) owing to the proximity of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and the long-lasting Arabic cultural and linguistic presence, mainly in Southern Iberia, during the [Al-Andalus](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus") era. [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese_language "Maltese language") is a Semitic language developed from a dialect of Arabic and written in the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet").[[22]](#cite_note-25) The [Balkan](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") languages, including [Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language"), [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian"), and [Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgarian_language "Bulgarian language"), have also acquired many words of Arabic origin, mainly through direct contact with [Ottoman Turkish](/wiki/Ottoman_Turkish "Ottoman Turkish"). Arabic has also [influenced other languages](/wiki/Influence_of_Arabic_on_other_languages "Influence of Arabic on other languages") across the globe throughout its history, especially languages where Islam had a historic presence. The most markedly influenced languages are [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"), [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") ([Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") and [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu")),[[23]](#cite_note-Versteegh19972-26) [Kashmiri](/wiki/Kashmiri_language "Kashmiri language"), [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurdish_language "Kurdish language"), [Bosnian](/wiki/Bosnian_language "Bosnian language"), [Kazakh](/wiki/Kazakh_language "Kazakh language"), [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") ([Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") and [Malaysian](/wiki/Malaysian_language "Malaysian language")), [Maldivian](/wiki/Maldivian_language "Maldivian language"), [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto"), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"), [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language"), [Azerbaijani](/wiki/Azerbaijani_language "Azerbaijani language"), [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language"), [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language"), [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_language "Sindhi language"), [Odia](/wiki/Odia_language "Odia language"),[[24]](#cite_note-Raya-1981-27) [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") and African languages such as [Hausa](/wiki/Hausa_language "Hausa language"), [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic"), [Tigrinya](/wiki/Tigrinya_language "Tigrinya language"), [Somali](/wiki/Somali_language "Somali language"), [Tamazight](/wiki/Tamazight "Tamazight"), and [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language"). Conversely, Arabic has borrowed some words (mostly nouns) from other languages, including its [sister-language](/wiki/Sister_language "Sister language") [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic"), Persian, Greek, and [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and to a lesser extent and more recently from [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), and [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language").

Classical Arabic (and Modern Standard Arabic) is considered a [conservative language](/wiki/Conservative_and_innovative_language "Conservative and innovative language") among Semitic languages; it preserved the complete [Proto-Semitic](/wiki/Proto-Semitic_language "Proto-Semitic language") three grammatical cases and [declension (ʾiʿrāb)](/wiki/%CA%BEI%CA%BFrab "ʾIʿrab"), and it was used in the reconstruction of Proto-Semitic since it preserves as contrastive 28 out of the evident 29 consonantal phonemes.[[25]](#cite_note-Versteegh199722-28)

## Classification

Further information: [Classification of Arabic languages](/wiki/Classification_of_Arabic_languages "Classification of Arabic languages")

Arabic is usually classified as a [Central Semitic language](/wiki/Central_Semitic_languages "Central Semitic languages"). Linguists still differ as to the best classification of Semitic language sub-groups.[[26]](#cite_note-Stefan_Weninger_2011-29) The Semitic languages changed between [Proto-Semitic](/wiki/Proto-Semitic_language "Proto-Semitic language") and the emergence of Central Semitic languages, particularly in grammar. Innovations of the Central Semitic languages—all maintained in Arabic—include:

1. The conversion of the suffix-conjugated stative formation (*jalas-*) into a past tense.
2. The conversion of the prefix-conjugated [preterite](/wiki/Preterite "Preterite")-tense formation (*yajlis-*) into a present tense.
3. The elimination of other prefix-conjugated mood/aspect forms (e.g., a present tense formed by doubling the middle root, a [perfect](/wiki/Perfect_(grammar) "Perfect (grammar)") formed by [infixing](/wiki/Infix "Infix") a /t/ after the first root consonant, probably a jussive formed by a stress shift) in favor of new moods formed by endings attached to the prefix-conjugation forms (e.g., *-u* for indicative, *-a* for subjunctive, no ending for jussive, *-an* or *-anna* for energetic).
4. The development of an internal passive.

There are several features which Classical Arabic, the modern Arabic varieties, as well as the [Safaitic](/wiki/Safaitic "Safaitic") and [Hismaic](/wiki/Hismaic_(Old_Arabic) "Hismaic (Old Arabic)") inscriptions share which are unattested in any other Central Semitic language variety, including the [Dadanitic](/wiki/Dadanitic "Dadanitic") and [Taymanitic](/wiki/Taymanitic "Taymanitic") languages of the [northern Hejaz](/wiki/Hijaz "Hijaz"). These features are evidence of common descent from a [hypothetical ancestor](/wiki/Proto-language "Proto-language"), [Proto-Arabic](/wiki/Proto-Arabic "Proto-Arabic").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAl-Jallad2020a8-30)[[28]](#cite_note-31) The following features of Proto-Arabic can be reconstructed with confidence:[[29]](#cite_note-32)

1. negative particles *m* \* /mā/; *lʾn* \*/lā-ʾan/ to Classical Arabic lan
2. mafʿūl G-passive participle
3. [prepositions](/wiki/Preposition_and_postposition "Preposition and postposition") and adverbs *f*, *ʿn*, *ʿnd*, *ḥt*, *ʿkdy*
4. a subjunctive in -*a*
5. *t*-demonstratives
6. leveling of the -*at* [allomorph](/wiki/Allomorph "Allomorph") of the feminine ending
7. *ʾn* [complementizer](/wiki/Complementizer "Complementizer") and subordinator
8. the use of *f*- to introduce modal clauses
9. independent object pronoun in *(ʾ)y*
10. vestiges of [nunation](/wiki/Nunation "Nunation")

On the other hand, several Arabic varieties are closer to other Semitic languages and maintain features not found in Classical Arabic, indicating that these varieties cannot have developed from Classical Arabic.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBirnstiel2019368-33)[[31]](#cite_note-34) Thus, Arabic [vernaculars](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") do not descend from Classical Arabic:[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVersteegh2014172-35) Classical Arabic is a sister language rather than their direct ancestor.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAl-Jallad2020a8-30)

## History

### Old Arabic

Main article: [Old Arabic](/wiki/Old_Arabic "Old Arabic")

Arabia had a wide variety of Semitic languages in antiquity. The term "Arab" was initially used to describe those living in southern [Syria](/wiki/Syria_(region) "Syria (region)"), [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") and the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula"), as perceived by geographers from [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece").[[12]](#cite_note-Al-Jallad-15)[[33]](#cite_note-36) In the southwest, various [Central Semitic languages](/wiki/Central_Semitic_languages "Central Semitic languages") both belonging to and outside the [Ancient South Arabian](/wiki/Old_South_Arabian "Old South Arabian") family (e.g. Southern Thamudic) were spoken. It is believed that the ancestors of the [Modern South Arabian languages](/wiki/Modern_South_Arabian_languages "Modern South Arabian languages") (non-Central Semitic languages) were spoken in southern Arabia at this time. To the north, in the oases of northern [Hejaz](/wiki/Hejaz "Hejaz"), [Dadanitic](/wiki/Dadanitic "Dadanitic") and [Taymanitic](/wiki/Taymanitic "Taymanitic") held some prestige as inscriptional languages. In [Najd](/wiki/Najd "Najd") and parts of western Arabia, a language known to scholars as Thamudic C is attested.[[12]](#cite_note-Al-Jallad-15)

In eastern Arabia, inscriptions in a script derived from ASA attest to a language known as [Hasaitic](/wiki/Hasaitic "Hasaitic"). On the northwestern frontier of Arabia, various languages known to scholars as [Thamudic B](/wiki/Thamudic_B "Thamudic B"), Thamudic D, [Safaitic](/wiki/Safaitic "Safaitic"), and [Hismaic](/wiki/Hismaic "Hismaic") are attested. The last two share important [isoglosses](/wiki/Isogloss "Isogloss") with later forms of Arabic, leading scholars to theorize that Safaitic and Hismaic are early forms of Arabic and that they should be considered [Old Arabic](/wiki/Old_Arabic "Old Arabic").[[12]](#cite_note-Al-Jallad-15)

Linguists generally believe that "Old Arabic", a collection of related dialects that constitute the precursor of Arabic, first emerged during the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age").[[26]](#cite_note-Stefan_Weninger_2011-29) Previously, the earliest attestation of Old Arabic was thought to be a single 1st century CE inscription in [Sabaic script](/wiki/Sabaic "Sabaic") at [Qaryat al-Faw](/wiki/Qaryat_al-Faw "Qaryat al-Faw"), in southern present-day Saudi Arabia. However, this inscription does not participate in several of the key innovations of the Arabic language group, such as the conversion of Semitic [mimation](/wiki/Mimation "Mimation") to [nunation](/wiki/Nunation "Nunation") in the singular. It is best reassessed as a separate language on the Central Semitic dialect continuum.[[34]](#cite_note-37)

It was also thought that Old Arabic coexisted alongside—and then gradually displaced—[epigraphic](/wiki/Epigraphic "Epigraphic") [Ancient North Arabian](/wiki/Ancient_North_Arabian "Ancient North Arabian") (ANA), which was theorized to have been the regional tongue for many centuries. ANA, despite its name, was considered a very distinct language, and mutually unintelligible, from "Arabic". Scholars named its variant dialects after the towns where the inscriptions were discovered (Dadanitic, Taymanitic, Hismaic, Safaitic).[[26]](#cite_note-Stefan_Weninger_2011-29) However, most arguments for a single ANA language or language family were based on the shape of the definite article, a prefixed h-. It has been argued that the h- is an archaism and not a shared innovation, and thus unsuitable for language classification, rendering the hypothesis of an ANA language family untenable.[[35]](#cite_note-38) Safaitic and Hismaic, previously considered ANA, should be considered Old Arabic due to the fact that they participate in the innovations common to all forms of Arabic.[[12]](#cite_note-Al-Jallad-15)

The earliest attestation of continuous Arabic text in an [ancestor of the modern Arabic script](/wiki/Nabataean_alphabet "Nabataean alphabet") are three lines of poetry by a man named Garm(')allāhe found in [En Avdat, Israel](/wiki/Avdat "Avdat"), and dated to around 125 CE.[[36]](#cite_note-39) This is followed by the [Namara inscription](/wiki/Namara_inscription "Namara inscription"), an epitaph of the [Lakhmid](/wiki/Lakhmids "Lakhmids") king Imru' al-Qays bar 'Amro, dating to 328 CE, found at Namaraa, Syria. From the 4th to the 6th centuries, the Nabataean script evolved into the Arabic script recognizable from the early Islamic era.[[37]](#cite_note-40) There are inscriptions in an undotted, 17-letter Arabic script dating to the 6th century CE, found at four locations in Syria ([Zabad](/wiki/Zabad_inscription "Zabad inscription"), [Jebel Usays](/wiki/Jebel_Usays_inscription "Jebel Usays inscription"), [Harran](/wiki/Harran_inscription "Harran inscription"), [Umm el-Jimal](/wiki/Umm_el-Jimal "Umm el-Jimal")). The oldest surviving papyrus in Arabic dates to 643 CE, and it uses dots to produce the modern 28-letter Arabic alphabet. The language of that papyrus and of the Qur'an is referred to by linguists as "Quranic Arabic", as distinct from its codification soon thereafter into "[Classical Arabic](/wiki/Classical_Arabic "Classical Arabic")".[[26]](#cite_note-Stefan_Weninger_2011-29)

### Classical Arabic

Main article: [Classical Arabic](/wiki/Classical_Arabic "Classical Arabic")

In late pre-Islamic times, a transdialectal and transcommunal variety of Arabic emerged in the [Hejaz](/wiki/Hejaz "Hejaz"), which continued living its parallel life after literary Arabic had been institutionally standardized in the 2nd and 3rd century of the [Hijra](/wiki/Islamic_calendar "Islamic calendar"), most strongly in Judeo-Christian texts, keeping alive ancient features eliminated from the "learned" tradition (Classical Arabic).[[38]](#cite_note-41) This variety and both its classicizing and "lay" iterations have been termed Middle Arabic in the past, but they are thought to continue an [Old Higazi](/wiki/Old_Higazi "Old Higazi") register. It is clear that the orthography of the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran") was not developed for the standardized form of Classical Arabic; rather, it shows the attempt on the part of writers to record an archaic form of Old Higazi.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the late 6th century AD, a relatively uniform intertribal "poetic koine" distinct from the [spoken vernaculars](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") developed based on the [Bedouin](/wiki/Bedouin "Bedouin") dialects of [Najd](/wiki/Najd "Najd"), probably in connection with the court of [al-Ḥīra](/wiki/Al-Hirah "Al-Hirah"). During the first Islamic century, the majority of Arabic poets and Arabic-writing persons spoke Arabic as their mother tongue. Their texts, although mainly preserved in far later manuscripts, contain traces of non-standardized [Classical Arabic](/wiki/Classical_Arabic "Classical Arabic") elements in morphology and syntax.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Standardization

[Abu al-Aswad al-Du'ali](/wiki/Abu_al-Aswad_al-Du%27ali "Abu al-Aswad al-Du'ali") (c. 603–689) is credited with standardizing [Arabic grammar](/wiki/Arabic_grammar "Arabic grammar"), or *an-naḥw* (النَّحو "the way"[[39]](#cite_note-42)), and pioneering a system of [diacritics](/wiki/Arabic_diacritics "Arabic diacritics") to differentiate consonants (نقط الإعجام *nuqaṭu‿l-i'jām* "pointing for non-Arabs") and indicate [vocalization](/wiki/Arabic_diacritics#Tashkil_(marks_used_as_phonetic_guides) "Arabic diacritics") (التشكيل *at-tashkīl*).[[40]](#cite_note-43) [Al-Khalil ibn Ahmad al-Farahidi](/wiki/Al-Khalil_ibn_Ahmad_al-Farahidi "Al-Khalil ibn Ahmad al-Farahidi") (718–786) compiled the first Arabic dictionary, *[Kitāb al-'Ayn](/wiki/Kitab_al-%27Ayn "Kitab al-'Ayn")* (كتاب العين "The Book of the Letter [ع](/wiki/Ayin "Ayin")"), and is credited with establishing the rules of Arabic [prosody](/wiki/Prosody_(linguistics) "Prosody (linguistics)").[[41]](#cite_note-44) [Al-Jahiz](/wiki/Al-Jahiz "Al-Jahiz") (776–868) proposed to [Al-Akhfash al-Akbar](/wiki/Al-Akhfash_al-Akbar "Al-Akhfash al-Akbar") an overhaul of the grammar of Arabic, but it would not come to pass for two centuries.[[42]](#cite_note-Landmarks_in_linguistic_thought_III-1997-45) The standardization of Arabic reached completion around the end of the 8th century. The first comprehensive description of the *ʿarabiyya* "Arabic", [Sībawayhi's](/wiki/Sibawayh "Sibawayh") *al*-*Kitāb*, is based first of all upon a corpus of poetic texts, in addition to Qur'an usage and Bedouin informants whom he considered to be reliable speakers of the *ʿarabiyya*.[[43]](#cite_note-referenceworks.brillonline.com-46)

### Spread

Further information: [Arabization](/wiki/Arabization "Arabization")

Arabic spread with the spread of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"). Following the [early Muslim conquests](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests"), Arabic gained vocabulary from [Middle Persian](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian") and [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language").[[44]](#cite_note-The_National-2016-47) In the early [Abbasid period](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate"), many [Classical Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") terms entered Arabic through translations carried out at [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad")'s [House of Wisdom](/wiki/House_of_Wisdom "House of Wisdom").[[44]](#cite_note-The_National-2016-47)

By the 8th century, knowledge of Classical Arabic had become an essential prerequisite for rising into the higher classes throughout the Islamic world, both for Muslims and non-Muslims. For example, [Maimonides](/wiki/Maimonides "Maimonides"), the [Andalusi](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus") Jewish philosopher, authored works in [Judeo-Arabic](/wiki/Judeo-Arabic_dialects "Judeo-Arabic dialects")—Arabic written in [Hebrew script](/wiki/Hebrew_alphabet "Hebrew alphabet").[[45]](#cite_note-48)

### Development

[Ibn Jinni](/wiki/Ibn_Jinni "Ibn Jinni") of [Mosul](/wiki/Mosul "Mosul"), a pioneer in [phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology"), wrote prolifically in the 10th century on Arabic morphology and phonology in works such as *[Kitāb Al-Munṣif](/w/index.php?title=Kit%C4%81b_Al-Mun%E1%B9%A3if&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kitāb Al-Munṣif (page does not exist)"), [Kitāb Al-Muḥtasab](/w/index.php?title=Kit%C4%81b_Al-Mu%E1%B8%A5tasab&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kitāb Al-Muḥtasab (page does not exist)"), and* *[Kitāb Al-Khaṣāʾiṣ](/w/index.php?title=Kit%C4%81b_Al-Kha%E1%B9%A3%C4%81%CA%BEi%E1%B9%A3&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kitāb Al-Khaṣāʾiṣ (page does not exist)")* [[ar](https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AE%D8%B5%D8%A7%D8%A6%D8%B5_(%D9%83%D8%AA%D8%A7%D8%A8) "ar:الخصائص (كتاب)")].[[46]](#cite_note-49)

[Ibn Mada'](/wiki/Ibn_Mada%27 "Ibn Mada'") of [Cordoba](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Spain "Córdoba, Spain") (1116–1196) realized the overhaul of Arabic grammar first proposed by [Al-Jahiz](/wiki/Al-Jahiz "Al-Jahiz") 200 years prior.[[42]](#cite_note-Landmarks_in_linguistic_thought_III-1997-45)

The Maghrebi lexicographer [Ibn Manzur](/wiki/Ibn_Manzur "Ibn Manzur") compiled *[Lisān al-ʿArab](/wiki/Lis%C4%81n_al-%CA%BFArab "Lisān al-ʿArab")* (لسان العرب, "Tongue of Arabs"), a major reference [dictionary](/wiki/Dictionary "Dictionary") of Arabic, in 1290.[[47]](#cite_note-50)

### Neo-Arabic

[Charles Ferguson](/wiki/Charles_A._Ferguson "Charles A. Ferguson")'s [koine](/wiki/Koin%C3%A9_language "Koiné language") theory claims that the modern Arabic dialects collectively descend from a single military koine that sprang up during the Islamic conquests; this view has been challenged in recent times. Ahmad al-Jallad proposes that there were at least two considerably distinct types of Arabic on the eve of the conquests: Northern and Central (Al-Jallad 2009). The modern dialects emerged from a new contact situation produced following the conquests. Instead of the emergence of a single or multiple koines, the dialects contain several sedimentary layers of borrowed and areal features, which they absorbed at different points in their linguistic histories.[[43]](#cite_note-referenceworks.brillonline.com-46) According to Veersteegh and Bickerton, colloquial Arabic dialects arose from [pidginized](/wiki/Pidgin "Pidgin") Arabic formed from contact between Arabs and conquered peoples. Pidginization and subsequent [creolization](/wiki/Creolization "Creolization") among Arabs and [arabized](/wiki/Arabization "Arabization") peoples could explain relative morphological and phonological simplicity of vernacular Arabic compared to Classical and MSA.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVersteegh2014299-51)[[49]](#cite_note-52)

In around the 11th and 12th centuries in [al-Andalus](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus"), the *[zajal](/wiki/Zajal "Zajal")* and [*muwashah*](/wiki/Muwashshah "Muwashshah") poetry forms developed in the [dialectical Arabic of Cordoba](/wiki/Andalusian_Arabic "Andalusian Arabic") and the Maghreb.[[50]](#cite_note-53)

### Nahda

The *[Nahda](/wiki/Nahda "Nahda")* was a cultural and especially literary renaissance of the 19th century in which writers sought "to fuse Arabic and European forms of expression."[[51]](#cite_note-Gelvin-2020-54) According to [James L. Gelvin](/wiki/James_L._Gelvin "James L. Gelvin"), "*Nahda* writers attempted to simplify the Arabic language and script so that it might be accessible to a wider audience."[[51]](#cite_note-Gelvin-2020-54)

In the wake of the [industrial revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") and European [hegemony](/wiki/Hegemony "Hegemony") and [colonialism](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism"), pioneering Arabic presses, such as the [Amiri Press](/wiki/Amiri_Press "Amiri Press") established by [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_of_Egypt "Muhammad Ali of Egypt") (1819), dramatically changed the diffusion and consumption of [Arabic literature](/wiki/Arabic_literature "Arabic literature") and publications.[[52]](#cite_note-55) [Rifa'a al-Tahtawi](/wiki/Rifa%27a_al-Tahtawi "Rifa'a al-Tahtawi") proposed the establishment of [Madrasat al-Alsun](/wiki/Madrasat_al-Alsun "Madrasat al-Alsun") in 1836 and led a translation campaign that highlighted the need for a lexical injection in Arabic, to suit concepts of the industrial and post-industrial age (such as *sayyārah* سَيَّارَة 'automobile' or *bākhirah* باخِرة 'steamship').[[53]](#cite_note-56)[[54]](#cite_note-57)

In response, a number of Arabic academies modeled after the *[Académie française](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_fran%C3%A7aise "Académie française")* were established with the aim of developing standardized additions to the Arabic lexicon to suit these transformations,[[55]](#cite_note-58) first in [Damascus](/wiki/Arab_Academy_of_Damascus "Arab Academy of Damascus") (1919), then in [Cairo](/wiki/Academy_of_the_Arabic_Language_in_Cairo "Academy of the Arabic Language in Cairo") (1932), [Baghdad](/wiki/Iraqi_Academy_of_Sciences "Iraqi Academy of Sciences") (1948), [Rabat](/wiki/Institute_for_Studies_and_Research_on_Arabization "Institute for Studies and Research on Arabization") (1960), [Amman](/wiki/Jordan_Academy_of_Arabic "Jordan Academy of Arabic") (1977), [Khartum](/w/index.php?title=Academy_of_the_Arabic_Language_in_Khartum&action=edit&redlink=1 "Academy of the Arabic Language in Khartum (page does not exist)") [[ar](https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D9%85%D8%AC%D9%85%D8%B9_%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%84%D8%BA%D8%A9_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B9%D8%B1%D8%A8%D9%8A%D8%A9_%D8%A8%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AE%D8%B1%D8%B7%D9%88%D9%85 "ar:مجمع اللغة العربية بالخرطوم")] (1993), and [Tunis](/wiki/Tunisian_Academy_of_Sciences,_Letters,_and_Arts "Tunisian Academy of Sciences, Letters, and Arts") (1993).[[56]](#cite_note-UNESCO-2019-59) They review language development, monitor new words and approve the inclusion of new words into their published standard dictionaries.

In 1997, a bureau of Arabization standardization was added to the [Educational, Cultural, and Scientific Organization](/wiki/Arab_League_Educational,_Cultural_and_Scientific_Organization "Arab League Educational, Cultural and Scientific Organization") of the [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League").[[56]](#cite_note-UNESCO-2019-59) These academies and organizations have worked toward the [Arabization](/wiki/Arabization "Arabization") of the sciences, [creating terms](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism") in Arabic to describe new concepts, toward the standardization of these new terms throughout the Arabic-speaking world, and toward the development of Arabic as a [world language](/wiki/World_language "World language").[[56]](#cite_note-UNESCO-2019-59) This gave rise to what Western scholars call Modern Standard Arabic. From the 1950s, [Arabization](/wiki/Arabization "Arabization") became a postcolonial nationalist policy in countries such as Tunisia, Algeria, Morocco,[[57]](#cite_note-60) and Sudan.[[58]](#cite_note-61)

## Classical, Modern Standard and spoken Arabic

Further information: [Classical Arabic](/wiki/Classical_Arabic "Classical Arabic"), [Modern Standard Arabic](/wiki/Modern_Standard_Arabic "Modern Standard Arabic"), and [Varieties of Arabic](/wiki/Varieties_of_Arabic "Varieties of Arabic")

See also: [List of Arabic dictionaries](/wiki/List_of_Arabic_dictionaries "List of Arabic dictionaries")

*Arabic* usually refers to Standard Arabic, which Western linguists divide into [Classical Arabic](/wiki/Classical_Arabic "Classical Arabic") and Modern Standard Arabic.[[59]](#cite_note-Kamusella-62) It could also refer to any of a variety of regional vernacular [Arabic dialects](/wiki/Varieties_of_Arabic "Varieties of Arabic"), which are not necessarily mutually intelligible.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Safaitic_script_with_a_figure_of_a_camel_on_a_red_sandstone_fragment%2C_from_es-Safa%2C_currently_housed_in_the_British_Museum.jpg/250px-Safaitic_script_with_a_figure_of_a_camel_on_a_red_sandstone_fragment%2C_from_es-Safa%2C_currently_housed_in_the_British_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Safaitic_script_with_a_figure_of_a_camel_on_a_red_sandstone_fragment,_from_es-Safa,_currently_housed_in_the_British_Museum.jpg)

[Safaitic](/wiki/Safaitic "Safaitic") inscription

Classical Arabic is the language found in the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran"), used from the period of [Pre-Islamic Arabia](/wiki/Pre-Islamic_Arabia "Pre-Islamic Arabia") to that of the [Abbasid Caliphate](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate"). Classical Arabic is prescriptive, according to the [syntactic](/wiki/Syntactic "Syntactic") and grammatical norms laid down by classical grammarians (such as [Sibawayh](/wiki/Sibawayh "Sibawayh")) and the vocabulary defined in classical dictionaries (such as the [*Lisān al-ʻArab*](/wiki/Ibn_Manzur#Lisān_al-ʿArab "Ibn Manzur")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) largely follows the grammatical standards of Classical Arabic and uses much of the same vocabulary. However, it has discarded some grammatical constructions and vocabulary that no longer have any counterpart in the spoken varieties and has adopted certain new constructions and vocabulary from the spoken varieties. Much of the new vocabulary is used to denote concepts that have arisen in the [industrial](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") and [post-industrial era](/wiki/Post-industrial_society "Post-industrial society"), especially in modern times.[[60]](#cite_note-auto-63)

Due to its grounding in Classical Arabic, Modern Standard Arabic is removed over a millennium from everyday speech, which is construed as a multitude of dialects of this language. These dialects and Modern Standard Arabic are described by some scholars as not mutually comprehensible. The former are usually acquired in families, while the latter is taught in formal education settings. However, there have been studies reporting some degree of comprehension of stories told in the standard variety among preschool-aged children.[[60]](#cite_note-auto-63)

The relation between Modern Standard Arabic and these dialects is sometimes compared to that of [Classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin") and [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") vernaculars (which became [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages")) in medieval and early modern Europe.[[59]](#cite_note-Kamusella-62)

MSA is the variety used in most current, printed Arabic publications, spoken by some of the Arabic media across North Africa and the Middle East, and understood by most educated Arabic speakers. "Literary Arabic" and "Standard Arabic" (فُصْحَى **fuṣḥá**) are less strictly defined terms that may refer to Modern Standard Arabic or Classical Arabic.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some of the differences between Classical Arabic (CA) and Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) are as follows:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* Certain grammatical constructions of CA that have no counterpart in any modern vernacular dialect (e.g., the [energetic mood](/wiki/Energetic_mood "Energetic mood")) are almost never used in Modern Standard Arabic.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Case](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case") distinctions are very rare in Arabic vernaculars. As a result, MSA is generally composed without case distinctions in mind, and the proper cases are added after the fact, when necessary. Because most case endings are noted using final short vowels, which are normally left unwritten in the Arabic script, it is unnecessary to determine the proper case of most words. The practical result of this is that MSA, like English and [Standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"), is written in a strongly determined word order and alternative orders that were used in CA for emphasis are rare. In addition, because of the lack of case marking in the spoken varieties, most speakers cannot consistently use the correct endings in extemporaneous speech. As a result, spoken MSA tends to drop or regularize the endings except when reading from a prepared text.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* The numeral system in CA is complex and heavily tied in with the case system. This system is never used in MSA, even in the most formal of circumstances; instead, a greatly simplified system is used, approximating the system of the conservative spoken varieties.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Arabic [Swadesh list](/wiki/Swadesh_list "Swadesh list") (1–100)

MSA uses much Classical vocabulary (e.g., **dhahaba** 'to go') that is not present in the spoken varieties, but deletes Classical words that sound obsolete in MSA. In addition, MSA has borrowed or coined many terms for concepts that did not exist in Quranic times, and MSA continues to evolve.[[61]](#cite_note-64) Some words have been borrowed from other languages—notice that transliteration mainly indicates spelling and not real pronunciation (e.g., فِلْم **film** 'film' or ديمقراطية **dīmuqrāṭiyyah** 'democracy').[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The current preference is to avoid direct borrowings, preferring to either use [loan translations](/wiki/Loan_translation "Loan translation") (e.g., فرع **farʻ** 'branch', also used for the branch of a company or organization; جناح **janāḥ** 'wing', is also used for the wing of an airplane, building, air force, etc.), or to coin new words using forms within existing [roots](/wiki/Semitic_root "Semitic root") (استماتة **istimātah** '[apoptosis](/wiki/Apoptosis "Apoptosis")', using the root موت *m/w/t* 'death' put into the [Xth form](/wiki/Arabic_verbs#Formation_of_derived_stems_("forms") "Arabic verbs"), or جامعة **jāmiʻah** 'university', based on جمع **jamaʻa** 'to gather, unite'; جمهورية **jumhūriyyah** 'republic', based on جمهور **jumhūr** 'multitude'). An earlier tendency was to redefine an older word although this has fallen into disuse (e.g., هاتف **hātif** 'telephone' < 'invisible caller (in Sufism)'; جريدة **jarīdah** 'newspaper' < 'palm-leaf stalk').[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

*Colloquial* or *dialectal* Arabic refers to the many national or regional varieties which constitute the everyday spoken language. Colloquial Arabic has many regional variants; geographically distant varieties usually differ enough to be [mutually unintelligible](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility"), and some linguists consider them distinct languages.[[62]](#cite_note-65) However, research indicates a high degree of mutual intelligibility between closely related Arabic variants for native speakers listening to words, sentences, and texts; and between more distantly related dialects in interactional situations.[[63]](#cite_note-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Epitaph_Imru-l-Qays_Louvre_AO4083.jpg/250px-Epitaph_Imru-l-Qays_Louvre_AO4083.jpg)](/wiki/File:Epitaph_Imru-l-Qays_Louvre_AO4083.jpg)

The [Namara inscription](/wiki/Namara_inscription "Namara inscription"), a sample of [Nabataean script](/wiki/Nabataean_alphabet "Nabataean alphabet"), considered a direct precursor of Arabic script[[44]](#cite_note-The_National-2016-47)[[64]](#cite_note-67)

The varieties are typically unwritten. They are often used in informal spoken media, such as [soap operas](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera") and [talk shows](/wiki/Talk_show "Talk show"),[[65]](#cite_note-Jenkins-68) as well as occasionally in certain forms of written media such as poetry and printed advertising.

[Hassaniya Arabic](/wiki/Hassaniya_Arabic "Hassaniya Arabic"), [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese_language "Maltese language"), and [Cypriot Arabic](/wiki/Cypriot_Arabic "Cypriot Arabic") are only varieties of modern Arabic to have acquired official recognition.[[66]](#cite_note-69) Hassaniya is official in Mali[[67]](#cite_note-70) and recognized as a minority language in Morocco,[[68]](#cite_note-71) while the Senegalese government adopted the Latin script to write it.[[10]](#cite_note-HassaniyaAlphabet-12) Maltese is official in (predominantly [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church")) [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") and written with the [Latin script](/wiki/Maltese_alphabet "Maltese alphabet"). Linguists agree that it is a variety of spoken Arabic, descended from [Siculo-Arabic](/wiki/Siculo-Arabic "Siculo-Arabic"), though it has experienced extensive changes as a result of sustained and intensive contact with Italo-Romance varieties, and more recently also with English. Due to "a mix of social, cultural, historical, political, and indeed linguistic factors", many Maltese people today consider their language Semitic but not a type of Arabic.[[69]](#cite_note-Čéplö-72) Cypriot Arabic is recognized as a minority language in Cyprus.[[70]](#cite_note-73)

## Status and usage

### Diglossia

The sociolinguistic situation of Arabic in modern times provides a prime example of the linguistic phenomenon of [diglossia](/wiki/Diglossia "Diglossia"), which is the normal use of two separate varieties of the same language, usually in different social situations. *Tawleed* is the process of giving a new shade of meaning to an old classical word. For example, *al-hatif* lexicographically means the one whose sound is heard but whose person remains unseen. Now the term *al-hatif* is used for a telephone. Therefore, the process of *tawleed* can express the needs of modern civilization in a manner that would appear to be originally Arabic.[[71]](#cite_note-GUP-74)

In the case of Arabic, educated Arabs of any nationality can be assumed to speak both their school-taught Standard Arabic as well as their native dialects, which depending on the region may be mutually unintelligible.[[72]](#cite_note-75)[[73]](#cite_note-76)[[74]](#cite_note-77)[[75]](#cite_note-78)[[76]](#cite_note-79) Some of these dialects can be considered to constitute separate languages which may have "sub-dialects" of their own.[[77]](#cite_note-80) When educated Arabs of different dialects engage in conversation (for example, a Moroccan speaking with a Lebanese), many speakers [code-switch](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching") back and forth between the dialectal and standard varieties of the language, sometimes even within the same sentence.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Flag_of_the_Arab_League.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_Arab_League.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_Arab_League.svg)

Flag of the [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League"), used in some cases for the Arabic language

The issue of whether Arabic is one language or many languages is politically charged, in the same way it is for the [varieties of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese"), [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi_language "Hindi language") and [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu_language "Urdu language"), [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language "Serbian language") and [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"), [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language") and English, etc. In contrast to speakers of Hindi and Urdu who claim they cannot understand each other even when they can, speakers of the varieties of Arabic will claim they can all understand each other even when they cannot.[[78]](#cite_note-81)

While there is a minimum level of comprehension between all Arabic dialects, this level can increase or decrease based on geographic proximity: for example, Levantine and Gulf speakers understand each other much better than they do speakers from the Maghreb. The issue of diglossia between spoken and written language is a complicating factor: A single written form, differing sharply from any of the spoken varieties learned natively, unites several sometimes divergent spoken forms. For political reasons, Arabs mostly assert that they all speak a single language, despite mutual incomprehensibility among differing spoken versions.[[79]](#cite_note-82)

From a linguistic standpoint, it is often said that the various spoken varieties of Arabic differ among each other collectively about as much as the [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages").[[80]](#cite_note-83) This is an apt comparison in a number of ways. The period of divergence from a single spoken form is similar—perhaps 1500 years for Arabic, 2000 years for the Romance languages. Also, while it is comprehensible to people from the [Maghreb](/wiki/Maghreb "Maghreb"), a linguistically innovative variety such as [Moroccan Arabic](/wiki/Moroccan_Arabic "Moroccan Arabic") is essentially incomprehensible to Arabs from the [Mashriq](/wiki/Mashriq "Mashriq"), much as French is incomprehensible to Spanish or Italian speakers but relatively easily learned by them. This suggests that the spoken varieties may linguistically be considered separate languages.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Flag_of_Hejaz_%281917%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_Hejaz_%281917%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Hejaz_(1917).svg)

Flag used in some cases for the Arabic language (Flag of the [Kingdom of Hejaz](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hejaz "Kingdom of Hejaz") 1916–1925). The flag contains the four [Pan-Arab colors](/wiki/Pan-Arab_colors "Pan-Arab colors"): [black](/wiki/Black "Black"), [white](/wiki/White "White"), [green](/wiki/Green "Green") and [red](/wiki/Red "Red").

### Status in the Arab world in relation to other languages

With the sole exception of medieval linguist [Abu Hayyan al-Gharnati](/wiki/Abu_Hayyan_al-Gharnati "Abu Hayyan al-Gharnati") – who, while a scholar of the Arabic language, was not ethnically Arab – medieval scholars of the Arabic language made no efforts at studying comparative linguistics, considering all other languages inferior.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVersteegh2014107-84)

In modern times, the educated upper classes in the Arab world have taken a nearly opposite view. [Yasir Suleiman](/wiki/Yasir_Suleiman "Yasir Suleiman") wrote in 2011 that "studying and knowing English or French in most of the Middle East and North Africa have become a badge of sophistication and modernity and ... feigning, or asserting, weakness or lack of facility in Arabic is sometimes paraded as a sign of status, class, and perversely, even education through a mélange of code-switching practises."[[82]](#cite_note-85)

### As a foreign language

Arabic has been taught worldwide in many [elementary](/wiki/Elementary_school "Elementary school") and [secondary](/wiki/Secondary_school "Secondary school") schools, especially Muslim schools. Universities around the world have classes that teach Arabic as part of their foreign languages, [Middle Eastern studies](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_studies "Middle Eastern studies"), and [religious studies](/wiki/Religious_studies "Religious studies") courses. [Arabic language schools](/wiki/Arabic_language_school "Arabic language school") exist to assist students to learn Arabic outside the academic world. There are many Arabic [language schools](/wiki/Language_school "Language school") in the Arab world and other [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim_world "Muslim world") countries. Because the Quran is written in Arabic and all [Islamic terms](/wiki/Glossary_of_Islam "Glossary of Islam") are in Arabic, millions[[83]](#cite_note-86) of Muslims (both Arab and non-Arab) study the language.

Software and books with tapes are an important part of Arabic learning, as many of Arabic learners may live in places where there are no academic or Arabic language school classes available. Radio series of Arabic language classes are also provided from some radio stations.[[84]](#cite_note-87) A number of websites on the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") provide online classes for all levels as a means of distance education; most teach Modern Standard Arabic, but some teach regional varieties from numerous countries.[[85]](#cite_note-88)

## Vocabulary

### Lexicography

See also: [List of Arabic dictionaries](/wiki/List_of_Arabic_dictionaries "List of Arabic dictionaries")

#### Pre-modern Arabic lexicography

The tradition of Arabic [lexicography](/wiki/Lexicography "Lexicography") extended for about a millennium before the [modern period](/wiki/Modern_era "Modern era").[[86]](#cite_note-Encyclopaedia_of_Islam,_THREE-2020-89) Early lexicographers (لُغَوِيُّون *lughawiyyūn*) sought to explain words in the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran") that were unfamiliar or had a particular contextual meaning, and to identify words of non-Arabic origin that appear in the Quran.[[86]](#cite_note-Encyclopaedia_of_Islam,_THREE-2020-89) They gathered *shawāhid* (شَوَاهِد 'instances of attested usage') from [poetry](/wiki/Arabic_poetry "Arabic poetry") and the speech of the Arabs—particularly the Bedouin [ʾaʿrāb](/w/index.php?title=%CA%BEa%CA%BFr%C4%81b_(Bedouins)&action=edit&redlink=1 "ʾaʿrāb (Bedouins) (page does not exist)") [[ar](https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D8%A3%D8%B9%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%A8 "ar:أعراب")] (أَعْراب) who were perceived to speak the "purest," most eloquent form of Arabic—initiating a process of *jamʿu‿l-luɣah* (جمع اللغة 'compiling the language') which took place over the 8th and early 9th centuries.[[86]](#cite_note-Encyclopaedia_of_Islam,_THREE-2020-89)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Birmingham_Quran_manuscript_full.jpg/250px-Birmingham_Quran_manuscript_full.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birmingham_Quran_manuscript_full.jpg)

Arabic from the Quran in the old Hijazi dialect (Hijazi script, 7th century AD)

[*Kitāb al-'Ayn*](/wiki/Kitab_al-%27Ayn "Kitab al-'Ayn") (c. 8th century), attributed to [Al-Khalil ibn Ahmad al-Farahidi](/wiki/Al-Khalil_ibn_Ahmad_al-Farahidi "Al-Khalil ibn Ahmad al-Farahidi"), is considered the first [lexicon](/wiki/Dictionary "Dictionary") to include all [Arabic roots](/wiki/Semitic_root "Semitic root"); it sought to exhaust all possible root [permutations](/wiki/Permutation "Permutation")—later called *taqālīb* (تقاليب)*—*calling those that are actually used *mustaʿmal* (مستعمَل) and those that are not used *muhmal* (مُهمَل).[[86]](#cite_note-Encyclopaedia_of_Islam,_THREE-2020-89) [*Lisān al-ʿArab*](/wiki/Lisan_al-Arab "Lisan al-Arab") (1290) by [Ibn Manzur](/wiki/Ibn_Manzur "Ibn Manzur") gives 9,273 roots, while [*Tāj al-ʿArūs*](/wiki/Taj_al-%27Arus_min_Jawahir_al-Qamus "Taj al-'Arus min Jawahir al-Qamus") (1774) by [Murtada az-Zabidi](/wiki/Murtada_al-Zabidi "Murtada al-Zabidi") gives 11,978 roots.[[86]](#cite_note-Encyclopaedia_of_Islam,_THREE-2020-89)

This lexicographic tradition was traditionalist and corrective in nature—holding that linguistic correctness and eloquence derive from Qurʾānic usage, [pre-Islamic poetry](/w/index.php?title=Jahili_poetry&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jahili poetry (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Litt%C3%A9rature_pr%C3%A9islamique "fr:Littérature préislamique"); [ar](https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D8%A3%D8%AF%D8%A8_%D8%AC%D8%A7%D9%87%D9%84%D9%8A "ar:أدب جاهلي")], and Bedouin speech—positioning itself against *laḥnu‿l-ʿāmmah* (لَحْن العامة), the [solecism](/wiki/Solecism "Solecism") it viewed as defective.[[86]](#cite_note-Encyclopaedia_of_Islam,_THREE-2020-89)

#### Western lexicography of Arabic

In the second half of the 19th century, the British Arabist [Edward William Lane](/wiki/Edward_William_Lane "Edward William Lane"), working with the Egyptian scholar [Ibrāhīm Abd al-Ghaffār ad-Dasūqī](/w/index.php?title=Ibr%C4%81h%C4%ABm_Abd_al-Ghaff%C4%81r_ad-Das%C5%ABq%C4%AB&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ibrāhīm Abd al-Ghaffār ad-Dasūqī (page does not exist)") [[ar](https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D8%A5%D8%A8%D8%B1%D8%A7%D9%87%D9%8A%D9%85_%D8%B9%D8%A8%D8%AF_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%BA%D9%81%D8%A7%D8%B1_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AF%D8%B3%D9%88%D9%82%D9%8A "ar:إبراهيم عبد الغفار الدسوقي")],[[87]](#cite_note-90) compiled the *[Arabic–English Lexicon](/wiki/Arabic%E2%80%93English_Lexicon "Arabic–English Lexicon")* by translating material from earlier Arabic lexica into English.[[88]](#cite_note-91) The German Arabist [Hans Wehr](/wiki/Hans_Wehr "Hans Wehr"), with contributions from [Hedwig Klein](/wiki/Hedwig_Klein "Hedwig Klein"),[[89]](#cite_note-92) compiled the *[Arabisches Wörterbuch für die Schriftsprache der Gegenwart](/wiki/Arabisches_W%C3%B6rterbuch_f%C3%BCr_die_Schriftsprache_der_Gegenwart "Arabisches Wörterbuch für die Schriftsprache der Gegenwart")* (1952), later translated into English as *[A Dictionary of Modern Written Arabic](/wiki/A_Dictionary_of_Modern_Written_Arabic "A Dictionary of Modern Written Arabic")* (1961), based on established usage, especially in literature.[[90]](#cite_note-93)

#### Modern Arabic lexicography

The [Academy of the Arabic Language in Cairo](/wiki/Academy_of_the_Arabic_Language_in_Cairo "Academy of the Arabic Language in Cairo") sought to publish a [historical dictionary](/wiki/Historical_dictionary "Historical dictionary") of Arabic in the vein of the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")*, tracing the changes of meanings and uses of Arabic words over time.[[91]](#cite_note-94) A first volume of [*Al-Muʿjam al-Kabīr*](/wiki/Al-Mu%27jam_al-Kabir_(dictionary) "Al-Mu'jam al-Kabir (dictionary)") was published in 1956 under the leadership of [Taha Hussein](/wiki/Taha_Hussein "Taha Hussein").[[92]](#cite_note-95) The project is not yet complete; its 15th volume, covering the letter *[ṣād](/wiki/Tsade#Arabic_ṣād "Tsade")*, was published in 2022.[[93]](#cite_note-96)

### Loanwords

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Folio_Blue_Quran_Met_2004.88.jpg/250px-Folio_Blue_Quran_Met_2004.88.jpg)](/wiki/File:Folio_Blue_Quran_Met_2004.88.jpg)

The Qur'an has served and continues to serve as a fundamental reference for Arabic. ([Maghrebi](/wiki/Maghrebi_script "Maghrebi script") [Kufic](/wiki/Kufic "Kufic") script, [Blue Qur'an](/wiki/Blue_Qur%27an "Blue Qur'an"), 9th–10th century.)

The most important sources of borrowings into (pre-Islamic) Arabic are from the related (Semitic) languages [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic_language "Aramaic language"),[[94]](#cite_note-97) which used to be the principal, international language of communication throughout the ancient Near and Middle East, and [Ethiopic](/wiki/Ge%27ez_language "Ge'ez language"). Many cultural, religious and political terms have entered Arabic from [Iranian languages](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages"), notably [Middle Persian](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian"), [Parthian](/wiki/Parthian_language "Parthian language"), and (Classical) Persian,[[95]](#cite_note-98) and Hellenistic Greek (*kīmiyāʼ* has as origin the Greek *khymia*, meaning in that language the melting of metals; see [Roger Dachez](/wiki/Roger_Dachez "Roger Dachez"), *Histoire de la Médecine de l'Antiquité au XXe siècle*, Tallandier, 2008, p. 251), *alembic* (distiller) from *ambix* (cup), *almanac* (climate) from *almenichiakon* (calendar).

For the origin of the last three borrowed words, see Alfred-Louis de Prémare, *Foundations of Islam*, Seuil, L'Univers Historique, 2002. Some Arabic borrowings from Semitic or Persian languages are, as presented in De Prémare's above-cited book: [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* *madīnah*/[medina](/wiki/Medina "Medina") (مدينة, city or city square), a word of Aramaic origin ܡܕ݂ܝܼܢ݇ܬܵܐ *məḏī(n)ttā* (in which it means "state/city").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* *jazīrah* (جزيرة), as in the well-known form الجزيرة "Al-Jazeera", means "island" and has its origin in the Syriac ܓܵܙܲܪܬܵܐ *gāzartā*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* *lāzaward* (لازورد) is taken from Persian لاژورد *lājvard*, the name of a blue stone, lapis lazuli. This word was borrowed in several European languages to mean (light) blue – azure in English, *azur* in French and *azul* in Portuguese and Spanish.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Arabic_script_evolution.svg/250px-Arabic_script_evolution.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Arabic_script_evolution.svg)

Evolution of early [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script") (9th–11th century), with the *[Basmala](/wiki/Basmala "Basmala")* as an example, from [kufic](/wiki/Kufic "Kufic") *[*Qur'ān*](/wiki/Qur%27an "Qur'an")* manuscripts: (1) Early 9th century, script with no dots or diacritic marks;(2) and (3) 9th–10th century under the Abbasid dynasty, [Abu al-Aswad's](/wiki/Abu_al-Aswad_al-Du%27ali "Abu al-Aswad al-Du'ali") system established red dots with each arrangement or position indicating a different short vowel; later, a second black-dot system was used to differentiate between letters like **fā'** and **qāf**; (4) 11th century, in [al-Farāhidi's](/wiki/Al-Khalil_ibn_Ahmad_al-Farahidi "Al-Khalil ibn Ahmad al-Farahidi") system (system used today) dots were changed into shapes resembling the letters to transcribe the corresponding long vowels.

A comprehensive overview of the influence of other languages on Arabic is found in Lucas & Manfredi (2020).[[96]](#cite_note-Lucas2020-99)

### Influence on other languages

The influence of Arabic has been most important in Islamic countries, because it is the language of the Islamic sacred book, the Quran. Arabic is also an important source of vocabulary for languages such as [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic_language "Amharic language"), [Azerbaijani](/wiki/Azerbaijani_language "Azerbaijani language"), [Baluchi](/wiki/Baluchi_language "Baluchi language"), [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), [Berber](/wiki/Berber_languages "Berber languages"), [Bosnian](/wiki/Bosnian_language "Bosnian language"), [Chaldean](/wiki/Chaldean_Neo-Aramaic "Chaldean Neo-Aramaic"), [Chechen](/wiki/Chechen_language "Chechen language"), [Chittagonian](/wiki/Chittagonian_language "Chittagonian language"), [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"), [Dagestani](/wiki/Dagestani_language "Dagestani language"), [Dhivehi](/wiki/Maldivian_language "Maldivian language"), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language"), [Hausa](/wiki/Hausa_language "Hausa language"), [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"), [Kazakh](/wiki/Kazakh_language "Kazakh language"), [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurdish_language "Kurdish language"), [Kutchi](/wiki/Kutchi_Language "Kutchi Language"), [Kyrgyz](/wiki/Kyrgyz_language "Kyrgyz language"), [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") ([Malaysian](/wiki/Malaysian_language "Malaysian language") and [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language")), [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language"), [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"), [Rohingya](/wiki/Rohingya_language "Rohingya language"), [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), etc.) [Saraiki](/wiki/Saraiki_language "Saraiki language"), [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_language "Sindhi language"), [Somali](/wiki/Somali_language "Somali language"), [Sylheti](/wiki/Sylheti_language "Sylheti language"), [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language"), [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language"), [Tigrinya](/wiki/Tigrinya_language "Tigrinya language"), [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"), [Turkmen](/wiki/Turkmen_language "Turkmen language"), [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"), [Uyghur](/wiki/Uyghur_language "Uyghur language"), [Uzbek](/wiki/Uzbek_language "Uzbek language"), [Visayan](/wiki/Visayan_languages "Visayan languages") and [Wolof](/wiki/Wolof_language "Wolof language"), as well as other languages in countries where these languages are spoken.[[96]](#cite_note-Lucas2020-99) [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew") has been also influenced by Arabic especially during the process of [revival](/wiki/Revival_of_the_Hebrew_language "Revival of the Hebrew language"), as [MSA](/wiki/Modern_Standard_Arabic "Modern Standard Arabic") was used as a source for modern Hebrew vocabulary and roots.[[97]](#cite_note-100)

[English](/wiki/English_language "English language") has many Arabic loanwords, some directly, but most via other Mediterranean languages. Examples of such words include *admiral, adobe, alchemy, alcohol, algebra, algorithm, alkaline, almanac, amber, arsenal, assassin, candy, carat, cipher, coffee, cotton, ghoul, hazard, jar, kismet, lemon, loofah, magazine, mattress, sherbet, sofa, sumac, tariff,* and *zenith*.[[98]](#cite_note-101) Other languages such as [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese_language "Maltese language")[[99]](#cite_note-102) and [Kinubi](/wiki/Kinubi "Kinubi") derive ultimately from Arabic, rather than merely borrowing vocabulary or grammatical rules.

Terms borrowed range from religious terminology (like [Berber](/wiki/Berber_languages "Berber languages") *taẓallit*, "prayer", from *[salat](/wiki/Salat "Salat")* (صلاة **ṣalāh**)), academic terms (like [Uyghur](/wiki/Uyghur_language "Uyghur language") *mentiq*, "logic"), and economic items (like English *coffee*) to [placeholders](/wiki/Placeholder_name "Placeholder name") (like [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") **fulano**, "so-and-so"), everyday terms (like [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") *lekin*, "but", or Spanish *taza* and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") **tasse**, meaning "cup"), and expressions (like [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") **a betzef**, "galore, in quantity"). Most Berber varieties (such as [Kabyle](/wiki/Kabyle_language "Kabyle language")), along with [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language"), borrow some numbers from Arabic. Most Islamic religious terms are direct borrowings from Arabic, such as صلاة (*ṣalāh*), "prayer", and إمام (*imām*), "prayer leader".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In languages not directly in contact with the [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world"), Arabic loanwords are often transferred indirectly via other languages rather than being transferred directly from Arabic. For example, most Arabic loanwords in Hindustani and [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language") entered through [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"). Older Arabic loanwords in [Hausa](/wiki/Hausa_language "Hausa language") were borrowed from [Kanuri](/wiki/Kanuri_language "Kanuri language"). Most Arabic loanwords in [Yoruba](/wiki/Yoruba_language "Yoruba language") entered through [Hausa](/wiki/Hausa_language "Hausa language").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Arabic words made their way into several West African languages as Islam spread across the Sahara. Variants of Arabic words such as كتاب *kitāb* ("book") have spread to the languages of African groups who had no direct contact with Arab traders.[[100]](#cite_note-103)

Since, throughout the Islamic world, Arabic occupied a position similar to that of [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") in Europe, many of the Arabic concepts in the fields of science, philosophy, commerce, etc. were coined from Arabic roots by non-native Arabic speakers, notably by [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic") and Persian translators, and then found their way into other languages. This process of using Arabic roots, especially in [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurdish_languages "Kurdish languages") and Persian, to translate foreign concepts continued through to the 18th and 19th centuries, when swaths of Arab-inhabited lands were under [Ottoman rule](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
